item.description: Writing and notes by Thom Shutt.

item.description: Given generative models of AI, we have already lost a battle of what is real in the internet.

item.description: A year of difficult calls, double leaps into the unknown, and starting over from inside Autonoma.

item.description: Design, Graphics, and Code

item.title: Sen Hongo
item.description: Sen Hongo is bootstrapping Millefeuille, a personal finance start up

item.title: Databases Were Not Designed For This
item.description: There is an implicit contract at the foundation of every database architecture decision you have ever made. You probably never wrote it down. Nobody does. It just… existed.

item.title: How did they get so good?
item.description: In two earlier posts I covered a bit of the history of the current batch of AI models, what they are good at, and what they’re not so good at. Had I published those posts a year earlier, we probably could have left the story there, but unless you’ve been living under a rock, it’s clear that the situation has evolved rather rapidly. I will try, then, with this post to conclude the story of how we got to the place we are now (early 2026) and to provide maybe a hint of where we are going.

item.title: How to Thought Lead (2026)
item.description: I first started compiling " How To Thought Lead (https://x.com/swyx/status/2028694299647656252)" in my notes 5 years ago, at first as an ironic parody and then slowly becoming sincere, and never published it, 1) because I don't know if I ever really nailed it / have a complete picture, 2) I was somewhat worried if I published it then others might diminish the techniques through overuse.

item.title: Designing an AI Gateway and Durable Workflow System | Steve Kinney
item.description: A two-layer architecture for production AI systems: a gateway that abstracts providers, enforces policies, and tracks costs, paired with a durable workflow engine that makes long-running agentic tasks survive failures, pause for human approval, and replay deterministically.

item.title: Surprising a model
item.description: Back when my kids were little and I was working at Google I was dogfooding an internal Maps or Google Now (remember that?) experiment on my Android phone that nudged you to get going for an event that it had inferred you needed to get to—even if it was not explicitly an event on your calendar! For me, this was a notification at 5:30 pm on weekdays to get moving to pick up kids from daycare before it closed at 6 pm. It understood this about my life with very high confidence after maybe a week of silent observation.

item.title: Migrating from DigitalOcean to Hetzner: From $1,432 to $233/month With Zero Downtime
item.description: A real-world production migration from DigitalOcean to Hetzner dedicated, handling 248 GB of MySQL data across 30 databases, 34 Nginx sites, GitLab EE, Neo4j, and live mobile app traffic — with zero downtime.

item.title: The best platform team ships zero tools
item.description: Instead of one dashboard that half the company tolerates, imagine twelve that each perfectly serve the people who asked for them. That’s where I think internal tooling is heading. As the cost of building software drops, the natural endpoint isn’t fewer, better applications. It’s many more, each built for a narrow audience, maybe even a single team or workflow. But that only works if building and deploying them is nearly free, and right now, it isn’t.

item.title: Who Watches the Provers? — Leonardo de Moura
item.description: Leonardo de Moura — Creator of Lean and Z3

item.title: Karpathy's Autoresearch Is Two Theses Running in a Loop
item.description: Karpathy's autoresearch demonstrates two ideas: cheap verification enables autonomous AI loops, and that same cheapness enables search through solution space at scale.

item.title: What Will Future Programming Languages Look Like?
item.description:
*If you would like to watch a video version of this post, check it out here.*
I’ve been getting into programming language development, compilers, and more...

item.title: Conversational Software Engineering: Compiling Intent
item.description: In a previous article, "AI Didn't Simplify Software Engineering", I argued that AI made bad engineering easier, not simpler. Code was never the hard part; the hard part is keeping intent, specification, tests, and implementation aligned. When that alignment goes, systems drift—and faster generation accelerates drift. Dijkstra, Hoare, Knuth, and others were already saying as much decades ago; the tools have changed, the problem hasn't.

item.title: Claude vs. a €12 Bluetooth keyboard on Linux - Jukka Niiranen blog
item.description: AI coding agents are great at solving pure software issues. How about hardware debugging? The observation gap is the real killer there.

item.description: If you don't have administrator rights on a Windows computer, you can still install and run .NET.

item.title: If you thought the speed of writing code was your problem - you have bigger problems | Debugging Leadership
item.description: AI coding tools are optimising the wrong thing and nobody wants to hear it. Writing code was already fast. The bottleneck is everything else: unclear requirements, review queues, terrified deploy cultures, and an org chart that needs six meetings to decide what colour the button should be.

item.title: A sufficiently detailed spec is code
item.description: Specifications do not address the limitations of agentic coding

item.description: A groundbreaking hack for Microsoft’s ‘unhackable’ Xbox One was revealed at the recent RE//verse 2026 conference.

item.title: Discriminated Unions Are Finally Here in .NET
item.description:
Get every Dometrain 30% off with code HANDSON30: https://dometrain.com/courses/?ref=nick-chapsas&promo=youtube&coupon_code=HANDSON30
Hello, everybody. I'm Nick, and in this video, I will showcase unions in C# 15 and .NET 11 since they have finally been merged in preview.
Don't forget to comment, like and subscribe :)
Social Media: Follow me on GitHub: https://github.com/Elfocrash Follow me on Twitter: https://twitter.com/nickchapsas Connect on LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/in/nick-chapsas
#csharp #dotnet

item.title: Reddit User Uncovers Who Is Behind Meta’s $2B Lobbying for Invasive Age Verification Tech
item.description: Meta funneled $2B through nonprofit shells to push age verification laws targeting Apple and Google while exempting its own platforms from surveillance requirements.

item.description: Kagi Translate uses powerful AI models to instantly and accurately translate any content in any language.

item.title: Rotational Gravity
Why isn’t anyone panicking?
item.description: The S&P 500 is near all-time highs. Crypto is up. And yet the US has started its biggest Middle East war since Iraq, and 20% of the world’s oil just went offline. Why is most everybody pretending things are normal?

item.title: One More Prompt: The Dopamine Trap of Agentic Coding
item.description: It’s 2:47 AM on a Tuesday. I’m not debugging a production outage. There’s no deadline. I’m just watching Claude Code refactor a module — and I can’t stop. One more prompt. One more agent run. One more hit. If this sounds familiar, you’re not alone. Something strange is happening to developers in 2026: AI coding tools may be triggering the same dopamine loops as slot machines, and the tech industry is sleepwalking into it.

item.title: Out of Sample Permutation Feature Importance For Random Forest’s Feature Optimization
item.description: After the first level of tuning of the random forest’s parameters, it came time to optimize the features on which the random forest gets trained on. I’ve already did a minor cleanup, but I didn’t...

item.title: Operating Systems: Timeline and Family Tree
item.description: Operating Systems: Timeline and Family Tree

item.title: Build Everything
item.description: Claude is changing how I write software dramatically. My biggest worry, as someone who's been writing software for a long time, is that I will be so blinded by my hard earned intuition on complexity that I will subconsciously be less ambitious than the moment calls for – that some treacherous part of my mind stubbornly knows it's too hard. When I was 25 I thought it was a given that I would just never stop learning. Back then the risk, from what I could see of middle aged developers, was clownishly bungling your way into some obvious technological cul-de-sac. The intervening 15 years have tested it at times, but I had not considered that the raw intuition itself might end up the problem.

item.title: Solving Problems By Writing Out Questions and Answers
item.description: Recently, I’ve been solving more and more complex problems. From working on those, I found a “strategy”/method that worked: to lay out the information needed as a bunch of questions, then answer them. I want to share the idea because I think it’s very useful in these ways: Clarification: creating questions and answers is writing, and it’s commonly known that writing makes your thinking clearer. It also eases your brain from keeping too many details at once 1.
